title: 2025/12/03 - MA (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-03 - Māori Affairs Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of Tupu Tonu—Ngāpuhi Investment Fund [Part 2]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of Tupu Tonu—Ngāpuhi Investment Fund
description: Wednesday 3 December 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee 2024/25 Annual review of Tupu Tonu—Ngāpuhi Investment Fund Hearing of evidence Submitters list: Tupu Tonu—Ngāpuhi Investment Fund Ben Dalton, Chair Lindsay Faithfull, Deputy Chair Geoff Taylor, Director Ripeka Evans, Director Sarah Petersen, Director Kelly Dunn, Chief Executive Kathy Mansell, Chief Investment Officer (Acting)
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of Tupu Tonu—Ngāpuhi Investment Fund. Tupu Tonu—Ngāpuhi Investment Fund. Ben Dalton, Chair

I, I'd be happy to answer that. Um, uh, been we, we are governed by, um, what we refer to as an investment policy statement. So the jargon that goes with that in funds management, often people talk about CY Os. Mm-hmm. Uh, so in our investment policy statement, it's driven first of all by an objective, and that objective is to outperform inflation.

So when we sit down and we consider investments that would fall into tupu, we consider they must pass that first criteria of, in our opinion, them being inflation adjusted in terms of the referred to intergenerational time horizon. So we then are more specific than that, that then we prescribe the objective that we are looking to, in that the portfolio should outperform CPI plus 2%.

So when we are looking at returns, as you have on your page here and the history of them, our objective, our key performance objective is to outperform inflation by 2%. Mm-hmm. So when you look at a return this year, that is like a 3% rounded out, um, uh, that's broadly consistent with only inflation, uh, and certainly a little below what is average across the period.

So let me come back therefore to the assets that you would expect to see us purchasing and those assets. Uh, if you look at the pie graph within the portfolio, um, land will commonly feature in any investment fund that is looking to outperform inflation. Uh, land tends to adjust satisfactorily for what is long, long term real value.

You may also note, uh, that our largest asset class remains cash. Mm-hmm. And our job, of course, is to convert that cash into portions of those other three pies. Our bias within our investment policy is towards infrastructure and energy, which is where we see both the most opportunity in the pipeline and the nicest fit for us in terms of getting adequate return against inflation.
